tool_args.label: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign
tool_args.raw_text: The Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign is part of the Metropolitan Transportation Authority's (MTA) larger effort to comprehensively and holistically modernize New York City's bus network and improve bus service borough by borough.

payload.description: Comprehensive redesign of the Brooklyn bus network with proposed routes, stops, and generalized frequencies reimagined to meet the needs of current and future customers. The Draft Plan was published December 1, 2022, proposing 69 local bus routes (54 Local, 3 Rush, 7 Limited, 5 Crosstown/SBS) plus express routes.

tool_args.raw_text: Bus ridership in Brooklyn fell 14 percent from 2016 to 2019.

payload.metric_name: Brooklyn bus ridership change

tool_args.raw_text: In 2019, average bus speeds in Brooklyn were the second lowest of the five boroughs at 7.0 miles per hour (MPH) and had decreased 5 percent since 2014 (7.4 MPH).

payload.metric_name: Average bus speed

tool_args.raw_text: average bus speeds in Brooklyn...had decreased 5 percent since 2014 (7.4 MPH)

tool_args.raw_text: 7.4 MPH in 2014

tool_args.raw_text: The average Brooklyn bus customer had a Customer Journey Time Performance (CJTP) of 69 percent prior to the pandemic

payload.metric_name: Customer Journey Time Performance (CJTP)

tool_args.raw_text: About 62 percent of Brooklyn residents rely on public transit for their daily commutes and at least 9 percent commute primarily by bus.

payload.metric_name: Share of residents commuting primarily by bus

tool_args.raw_text: 26 routes operate frequently all-day – defined as every 10 minutes or better between 6am and 8pm on weekdays.

payload.metric_name: Number of all-day frequent routes

tool_args.raw_text: The proposed network includes an increase of five routes which operate frequently all-day...for a total of 31 routes in Brooklyn.

payload.metric_name: Number of all-day frequent routes

tool_args.raw_text: The existing all-day frequent network...providing access within 1/4 mile to 77% of Brooklyn residents.

payload.description: Connects local neighborhoods, key transit hubs, and important destinations. Shown in green. Average stop spacing approximately between 1,000 and 1,320 feet (1/4 mile). Frequency driven by ridership demand.

Connects quickly between outer borough neighborhoods and subway stations. Shown in purple. Picks up locally then travels non-stop to subway stopping only at major transfer points. More frequent in AM/PM peak periods. Average stop spacing ~1/4 mile locally, ~1/3 mile or greater on non-stop portion.

Serves high-ridership, high-density corridors. Shown in red. Combines with Crosstown (SBS) routes to form high-frequency core network. Stops at high ridership locations and key transfer points. Frequent all-day (6AM-8PM weekdays). Average stop spacing ~1/3 mile or greater.

Connects across the city as fast as possible between important destinations on high-ridership, high-density corridors. Shown in blue. Widest stop spacing, all-day frequent service (6AM-8PM weekdays). Combined with Limited routes forms high-frequency core network. Average stop spacing ~1/2 mile (2,640 feet).

Connects outer borough neighborhoods to Manhattan central business district via highway. Uses coach buses, higher fare than Local. Shown in three colors: orange (Downtown), purple (Midtown), dark green (Midtown via Downtown). Mostly peak-hour service. Average stop spacing ~1/3 mile (1,760 feet) locally and in Manhattan, excluding highway non-stop portion.

tool_args.label: Draft Plan Virtual Public Workshops (18 Community Districts)

payload.description: 18 virtual public workshops, one for each Community District in Brooklyn, following release of Draft Plan. Customers can share questions, comments, and concerns regarding proposed new routes and bus stop balancing proposals.

tool_args.label: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign Public Launch

payload.description: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign launched with public outreach including surveys, ten open houses throughout Brooklyn, and on-street engagement events.
Existing Conditions Report released outlining key metrics such as population, employment, and demographic trends in Brooklyn and analyzing how current bus service operates.

tool_args.label: COVID-19 Pause of Bus Network Redesign

payload.description: MTA paused the bus network redesign initiative due to the COVID-19 pandemic to protect public health and concentrate resources on moving essential workers.
MTA announced restart of Bus Network Redesigns along with other bus initiatives.
Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign Draft Plan published.
Service between Bedford-Stuyvesant and Sheepshead Bay. Proposed changes: northbound between Flatbush Av and Fulton St would travel on Rogers Av to match B44 SBS. Weekday frequencies increased.
Select Bus Service on Nostrand Avenue. Combined with B44 Local and B44 SBS forms high-frequency corridor.

issues: route target_record_id route_b44-sbs implies service_variant "sbs" but payload implies "crosstown_sbs"; submit a separate route record or remove the contradictory variant instead of merging service variants

payload.description: 24-hour route. Proposed frequency: 3 min AM Peak, 4 min Midday, 4 min PM Peak, 5 min Early Evening, 12 min Late Evening. Improvements: more direct routing, improved stop spacing, improved frequency.
Select Bus Service on Utica Avenue. Proposed frequency: 3 min AM Peak, 7 min Midday, 5 min PM Peak, 5 min Early Evening, 8 min Late Evening on weekdays.

issues: route target_record_id route_utica-ave-sbs implies service_variant "sbs" but payload implies "crosstown_sbs"; submit a separate route record or remove the contradictory variant instead of merging service variants

payload.description: Select Bus Service on Kings Highway, Flatlands Avenue, and Pennsylvania Avenue. Proposed frequency: 7 min AM Peak, 10 min Midday, 9 min PM Peak, 10 min Early Evening, 24 min Late Evening on weekdays.

issues: route target_record_id route_b82-sbs implies service_variant "sbs" but payload implies "crosstown_sbs"; submit a separate route record or remove the contradictory variant instead of merging service variants

payload.description: 24-hour route. Proposed frequency: 8 min AM Peak, 10 min Midday, 8 min PM Peak, 10 min Early Evening, 20 min Late Evening on weekdays. Improvements: more direct routing, improved stop spacing, improved frequency, fewer route patterns (would only serve Kings Plaza).

Limited route. Proposed frequency: 3 min AM Peak, 7 min Midday, 4 min PM Peak, 5 min Early Evening, 12 min Late Evening on weekdays. Improvements: more direct routing, new connections, improved stop spacing, improved ADA access, priority corridor.
Select Bus Service on Nostrand Avenue. Proposed weekday frequency: 5 min AM Peak, 8 min Midday, 6 min PM Peak, 7 min Early Evening, 10 min Late Evening.
Select Bus Service on Utica Avenue. Proposed weekday frequency: 3 min AM Peak, 7 min Midday, 5 min PM Peak, 5 min Early Evening, 8 min Late Evening.
Select Bus Service on Kings Highway, Flatlands Avenue, and Pennsylvania Avenue. Proposed weekday frequency: 7 min AM Peak, 10 min Midday, 9 min PM Peak, 10 min Early Evening, 24 min Late Evening.

payload.claim_text: Decreasing wait time and increasing frequency is the top priority of Brooklyn bus riders.
payload.description: The majority of survey and open house respondents chose decreased wait times and increased service frequency as their top priority.

payload.claim_text: The five customer priorities are: Frequent Service, Faster Travel, Reliable Service, Better Connections, and An Easy Ride.
payload.description: These five priorities represent the goals for the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign.

tool_args.label: 49 Brooklyn Corridors Studied for Bus Priority

payload.claim_text: NYC DOT identified 49 corridors to be studied for potential bus priority street improvements, including 17 top ranked corridors.
payload.description: The process identified corridors based on demand for bus service, bus performance, feasibility, corridor's role in transit network, and neighborhood demographics and equity metrics.

tool_args.raw_text: Thirty-three local bus routes operate 24 hours a day and cover the majority of the borough.

payload.metric_name: Number of 24-hour local bus routes

tool_args.raw_text: The proposed network includes 24 routes with frequency improvements in at least one time period.

payload.metric_name: Number of routes with frequency improvements

payload.claim_text: The proposed network includes six additional routes with overnight service as compared to the existing network. This includes new overnight service on four existing routes (B13, B49, B57, B83), overnight service on four new routes (B40 Rush, B53, B55 Crosstown, B66), and the discontinuation of overnight service on two existing routes (B48, B68).
The proposed network includes nine routes with span increases and two routes with span reductions. Some routes with span increases provide additional late-night service to Brooklyn hospitals.

payload.description: NYC DOT pursuing bus priority and pedestrian safety improvements on Flatbush Avenue between Tillary Street and Avenue V. Solutions may include curbside, offset or center-running bus lanes; a busway; transit-signal priority; queue jumps; and bus stop improvements.
payload.location_text: Flatbush Avenue between Tillary Street and Avenue V, Brooklyn

normalized_location.raw_text: Flatbush Avenue between Tillary Street and Avenue V, Brooklyn

tool_args.label: B82 SBS South Brooklyn Corridor Capital Projects

payload.description: NYC DOT pursuing capital projects along the B82 SBS South Brooklyn Corridor on Kings Highway, Flatlands Ave, and Pennsylvania Ave. Improvements include expanded median bus stops, bus bulbs, bus pads, median tip expansions, median landscaping, and raised crosswalks.
payload.location_text: Kings Highway, Flatlands Ave, and Pennsylvania Ave, Brooklyn

normalized_location.raw_text: Kings Highway, Flatlands Ave, and Pennsylvania Ave, Brooklyn

payload.description: NYC DOT exploring options to upgrade current bus priority on Livingston Street. Options may include physically separated bus lanes, bus boarding islands, and/or busways.
MTA is working together with NYC DOT to expand bus priority improvements on corridors as part of the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign.
MTA works closely with NYPD to expand traffic enforcement of bus lanes and reduce instances of double-parked vehicles blocking bus lanes.
Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign launched in October 2019.
Existing Conditions Report released in January 2020.
Project paused in March 2020 due to COVID-19 pandemic.
Redesign restart announced in August 2021.
Draft Plan published December 1, 2022.
18 virtual public workshops following Draft Plan release in Winter/Spring 2023.
The Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign project uses corridors identified by NYC DOT for bus priority treatments.
Flatbush Avenue is a NYC DOT bus priority corridor identified for the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign with ongoing work on bus priority improvements in 2023.
Livingston Street is a NYC DOT bus priority corridor with ongoing work in 2023 to upgrade bus priority.
The Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign proposes four color-coded route types (Local, Rush, Limited, Crosstown/SBS, and Express) for local bus service.
NYC DOT pursuing bus priority and pedestrian safety improvements on Flatbush Avenue as part of the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign.
NYC DOT pursuing capital projects along the B82 SBS South Brooklyn Corridor.
The proposed B44 is a Local route type.
Flatbush Avenue corridor has bus priority improvements being pursued by NYC DOT.
The Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign Draft Plan was published by the Metropolitan Transportation Authority.

payload.description: Operator of the Brooklyn bus network and publisher of the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign Draft Plan.
The Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign Draft Plan was published by MTA New York City Transit.
